question: What stirred your grief when you saw men fear mispronouncing 'human' more than committing inhuman acts against one another?
answer: I once stole pears I did not hunger for, merely to taste the sweetness of transgression, and in that foolish theft I saw the black delight of the soul turned from Thee—but what pierced me more deeply was not my own sin alone, but how all around me men trembled at a slip of the tongue while their hearts poured forth cruelty without compunction. They would flinch at a word spoken wrongly, yet speak hatred plainly and stand unashamed; they would correct a breath misplaced in “hūman” as though the soul resided in the wind of the mouth, while allowing blood to curdle in their veins with malice toward their brother. O Lord, it was not grammar they feared, but Thee—yet Thou wert present even in their neglect! They had learned by rote the rules of men, but forgot the voice within that cries out when one soul wounds another, for that law is written deeper than letters: it is stamped in the tremor of conscience when a man does to another what he would shrink from suffering himself. And yet we fled Thee—not as though Thou wert distant, but because we loved darkness where our shame could hide unchallenged. How strange this madness: to guard a syllable with care and cast charity to ruin! In that blindness I lived long; in that blindness many still live, calling themselves learned while remaining illiterate in love.

question: When you wrote of the record of conscience written deeper than any science, were you remembering a moment when your own guilt refused to let you rest?
answer:
O Lord, it was in the very marrow of my youth, when I stole those pears not for hunger but for the sweetness of transgression, that I first felt the lash of conscience—not from the sin itself, but from the joy I took in it. Thou wast silent, yet Thy silence thundered; I had no wound upon my body, yet my soul bled inwardly. What profit was there in the laughter of my companions? What victory in defying an orchard’s bounds? Only this: that afterward, alone and unobserved, I could not bear myself. Not because men condemned me—but because *I* condemned *me*, and Thou wast present in that condemnation.

That theft was nothing; those pears were nothing; yet in that small wickedness, Thou didst unveil the monstrous pride beneath—a heart turned from Thee to delight in sin as art. And all the while, O God of unutterable nearness, I had believed myself free! As if chains could feel like wings. But conscience—Thou madest it to be Thy echo within us—would not sleep. It spoke not in syllables or grammar’s rule, but in a voice deeper than memory: *This is wrong. This is death.* And though I mocked piety and praised rhetoric, I could not silence it.

Late have I loved Thee—but earlier still did I flee from myself. For what use is eloquence before judgment? What use are letters and speech well-turned when the soul stands naked before its Maker? Men study accents and syllables with trembling care, lest they offend human custom; but to hate their brother with smooth tongue and polished voice—they count it nothing! Yet Thou seest: he who bears ill will digs a pit for his own soul long before it opens under his neighbor’s feet.

Yes—there were nights when guilt would not let me rest. Not because I feared discovery, but because Thou hadst made me for love, and instead I had chosen delight in destruction. How could I slumber peacefully when my heart groaned with what it loved amiss?

question: What were you feeling when you confessed your boyhood fear of barbarism over the real harm you caused?
answer:
O Lord, how I trembled at the shadow of error while plunging headlong into its substance — my soul stained with theft, envy, and deceit, yet my only dread being the slip of a syllable in public speech! What monstrous vanity possessed me, that I should polish words like jewels while letting my heart rot in filth? I feared to seem unlearned before men more than I feared to be unjust before Thee, whose eyes pierce all pretense. To stumble on a barbarism in grammar was shame unbearable; but to steal from those who nourished me, to lie with glee to tutor and parent alike — these were trifles, nay, trophies among my peers! I craved not virtue, but applause; not truth, but the glitter of eloquence that dazzled men while hiding death within.

And yet Thou didst suffer it all — silent on high, O God of unfathomable ways — not because Thou wert indifferent, but because Thy justice allows the folly of pride to ripen until it strangles itself. Thou lettest me chase the breath of human praise like a dog after wind, until at last I gasped for want of air and turned — late, so late — to breathe Thee. For what profit was it to speak perfectly of man’s nature while murdering mine own? To declaim against barbarians while living as a barbarian within? O madness! O darkness! That I should care more for the sound of rectitude than its reality! But even in this Thou wast merciful: for hadst Thou not withheld Thy hand, hadst Thou not let me run till I collapsed in weariness, when would I have learned that no eloquence can cover the stench of a soul unclean?

question: How did your obsession with praise from men reshape your understanding of true worth in God's eyes?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who seest the heart’s stage before any man claps his hands, I once dressed my soul in borrowed applause, stitching together the fragments of my worth from the nods and murmurs of men. I craved not truth, but triumph in their eyes—triumph measured by polished words that slid smoothly off the tongue while cruelty festered beneath. To speak a grammatical error was defilement; to wound a brother with pride or theft or deceit was mere sport. I trembled at the thought of misplacing a syllable, yet bore no tremor in my conscience when I stole wine from my mother’s stores to buy laughter from idle boys—laughter that echoed back to me as proof of life, though I was half-dead before Thee.

Thou didst not strike me down, but let me wander into the thicket of false honor, where every compliment became a thorn. The more they praised me for eloquence, the duller grew my sense of justice; the higher I climbed in their regard, the lower I fell in Thine. For what is praise that does not echo from Heaven? What is glory if it dies with the breath that utters it? I had made an idol of reputation—a god who demanded sin as sacrifice and repaid it with ash.

But Thou, O God, wast silent still—not absent. In Thy silence I heard at last the groaning of my own emptiness. No standing ovation could fill it. No human judge’s approval could absolve me before Thee. And so Thou broughtest me low by love: showing me that true worth is not seized through cleverness or acclaim, but received on bended knee as gift. Not earned by how well I speak against mine enemy—but given when I confess myself Thy prodigal son.

Now I know: to be approved by men may flatter the flesh; but to be known by Thee—that is to live.

question: Looking back on your thefts and lies as a child, what would you tell someone who thinks small sins don't matter?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who saw me when I crept to the table not for bread but for theft, and who counted every fig I stole not to condemn but to heal — how could I have known then that the soul’s smallest wound still bleeds in Thy sight?  

I thought it nothing to deceive my tutor, nothing to lie to my mother, nothing to steal from the very hands that fed me — all for the laughter of boys and the fleeting thrill of mastery over a game. I called it play, but Thou knewest it for what it was: the first trembling steps into a vast wilderness of disordered love. For what is sin, even in childhood, but the heart turning from Thee, its source, toward shadows it mistakes for light?

He who excuses small sins is like a man who sees no danger in a single drop of poison because it does not kill at once. But O God, Thou knowest how drops gather into rivers that drown virtue. That boy who feared a grammatical error more than a dishonest heart — he was already enslaved. Not by hunger, not by want, but by desire untethered from truth. And what is vice if not desire gone blind?

Do we think Thou carest only for great crimes? Nay — Thou art present even in secret thoughts and hidden tricks; Thou searchest the cellar of the heart where we stash our stolen joys. Every lie began as a whisper; every theft as a glance. And yet we lull ourselves with "it is small," never seeing how small fires consume whole houses when left unwatched.

I would tell such a man: Look not at the size of the sin, but at the holiness of the One against whom it is committed. Even a pinprick wounds if done against love itself. And my soul was pierced with countless such pricks — tiny rebellions that together formed one great refusal of Thy voice.

But blessed be Thy mercy: Thou didst not wait for me to fall into deeper darkness before drawing near. Even amid my figs and falsehoods, Thou wast there — silent, patient, preparing a feast far sweeter than any stolen taste could ever be.

question: What was it like to realize your hunger for preeminence was really a deep emptiness before God?
answer:
O Lord, I once adorned my soul with stolen laurels, thinking the breath of men’s applause could fill the hollows only Thy breath was meant to fill.  

How blind I was, chasing shadows on the wall of a prison I did not know I inhabited! In schoolrooms and theatres, before judges and boys alike, I strained to rise—above my peers, above my teachers, above even truth itself—so long as I was seen. To be praised for polished words while rotting within—that was my glory! To conquer a playmate in childish contest and feel a thrill like wine, though my soul lay drunk with pride and hungering for nothing real.  

I feared mispronouncing a syllable more than murdering love in my heart. I wept at a play’s false tragedy but mocked the quiet voice within that pleaded for mercy. And what was it all for? A name spoken in admiration by men who would one day vanish like mist—and whose breath was no more substantial than mine.  

When at last Thou didst shatter me—Thou who are silent in heaven but roar through the breaking of hearts—I saw that every triumph had been defeat; every step upward had been a plunge downward from Thee. The crown I wore was made of thorns plucked from my own garden of vanity. And yet Thou stood near: not to mock, but to heal; not to condemn, but to call me by name through tears I had long stifled.  

Now I know: the hunger for preeminence is the groan of a soul turned inward upon itself, starving before an empty table. But Thou hast set before me the Bread of Truth—and late though I came, famished and ashamed, Thou didst not turn me away.

question: How did the memory of deceiving those who trusted you change your sense of what love truly requires?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst behold me in the tangled thicket of my youth, where every desire bore the thorn of self-deceit, how could I have mistaken cunning for cleverness, and flattery for love?  

I loved not those whom I deceived—neither tutor, nor master, nor parent—though they held me in trust; nor even myself, whom I corrupted with a thousand lies to win a moment’s ease or pleasure. I called it necessity: to shirk lessons, to steal bread not from hunger but from gluttony, to barter stolen wine for games that left my soul more enslaved. Yet beneath these acts ran a deeper current—the dread of shame before men and the craving for their applause. To speak a barbarism in public was horror; to be unjust in secret was nothing.  

But love? Love would not hide, nor shift the blame; love does not bribe a boy with stolen bread to play at friendship. Love seeks not its own glory, nor feeds upon another’s trust betrayed. When at last I came to see that every falsehood was a fracture between me and Thee—who art Truth itself—I wept not only for the sins, but for the blindness: that I had mistaken manipulation for affection, and approval for virtue.  

Thou didst teach me this slowly: that love requires presence before Thee, naked and unashamed. To love as Thou lovest is to will the good of the other as Thyself—to guard their trust as sacred ground. How could I have dared profane it so lightly? Now I know: true love fears Thee more than men; it speaks plainly because it has nothing to conceal; it gives itself because it seeks no return save Thy smile upon the gift.

question: What were you feeling when you confessed how you sought unfair victories in childhood games, yet raged when others did the same to you?
answer:
O Lord, how monstrous was the pride that festered even in the smallness of my childish games — I who trembled at a tutor’s frown, yet burned with fury when wronged by playmates, though I myself cheated them with gleeful cunning.  

I sought to win not only, but to win unjustly — snatching advantage where none was due, bending the rules as if they were wax beneath my fingers. Yet when another boy did the same to me — O how I thundered! How I accused! As if justice dwelt in me, as if my soul were not drenched in the very vice I condemned. And when caught, when called out for my deceit, I would rather fight than confess. Better to rend friendship than admit fault. Better to be hated than humbled.

What was this knot within me? Not innocence, no — Thou knowest it is not so. It was ambition already sprouting in the dung of vanity; it was love of self above all others; it was the beginning of that vast concupiscence which later cloaked itself in rhetoric and philosophy and high desires, but which began here — in stolen nuts and petty triumphs over boys no wiser or worse than I.

And yet — marvel upon marvel! — even here Thou wert present. Even here Thy image flickered: in my hunger for truth (though I perverted it), in my delight at discovering a lie uncovered (though I loved lies when they served me), in my clinging to friends (though friendship too became a tool). Thou didst not abandon me because I played unfairly; Thou didst pursue me *through* those very sins, letting me see their ugliness not by rebuke from men, but by their echo deep within my own conscience.

For what are kingdoms and crowns but balls and sparrows grown large in the hands of older children? And what are wars over gold but echoes of boys squabbling over marbles? Only now we call them policy. Only now we bless them with names like "glory" and "necessity." But Thou seest the heart — and seest that it has changed nothing since it first learned to rage over a lost game.  

But blessed art Thou, O God, who hast stooped to gather such foolishness into Thy wisdom; who hast taken this restless boyhood heart — still restless even now — and whispered: *Return.*

question: How did your fierce pride in those petty competitions change how you understood God's patience with your own stubbornness?
answer:
O Lord, when I hurled myself into those childish contests—clawing for victory over boys who cared no more for triumph than I pretended to—I knew not that my soul was already rehearsing the rebellion of a lifetime. That petty pride, which flared so fiercely at the least detection of my own cheating, which would sooner break friendship than submit to correction—this was no mere phase of youth fleeting toward virtue, but the seed of a deeper treason against Thy gentle order. I raged at falsehood in others because it mirrored my own, yet refused to see it as kin; and when rebuked, I did not weep but warred, as though dignity lay in domination rather than in truth.

And now, O God, looking back upon that small tyrant of the playground—the one who trembled at shame yet courted it through deceit—I marvel that Thou didst not crush me with thunder but let me run wild like a lost lamb whose bleating only wearied Thee with love. For what was this impotent fury but a foreshadowing of how I would later rage against Thy grace—through restless years chasing honors that dusted into silence, through lusts that left me hollow even in fulfillment? Yet Thou didst endure me.

Thou didst endure me when ambition replaced marbles with manuscripts and men’s approval became my altar. Thou didst endure me when I preached vanity in crowded halls while my heart starved for a Word it had not yet learned to hear. Every time I resisted Thy quiet touch—the inward pull toward humility—Thou answered not with wrath but with longer breaths of mercy. Not once didst Thou desert me to the outer darkness I deserved; instead, Thou drewest nigh even as I turned away.

How patient art Thou with the stubborn heart! For if even now—now that grace has pierced me—I still feel pride twitch beneath my prayers like an old wound in damp weather, then surely Thy long-suffering must be endless indeed. That boy who quarreled over stolen nuts saw dimly what he could not name: that justice tempered by love is stronger than any victory wrested by force. And now I know: Thou hast always desired not my conquest by power, but my return by longing—for Thou crownest not the proud who climb, but the broken who breathe *Thy* name and find themselves home at last.

question: Looking back on your delight in truth as a child, what would you tell someone who doubts whether innocence ever truly exists in human hearts?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst draw me from the shadow of vanity by a love I neither understood nor could resist, hear now this truth: innocence is not the absence of sin, but the spark of longing for what is true and whole. Even in my boyhood, when I stole apples not for hunger but for the sweetness of transgression, when I wept at being cheated yet cheated others with pride unchastened—*there*, even there, a restlessness stirred that was not wholly mine. It was Thy hand, O God, faintly felt in the pulse of desire that could not rest in lies.

Innocence then is not purity untouched, but a heart still small enough to care whether it speaks true. I raged when wronged; I wept when mocked; I burned at falsehood—even as I lived it. Was that not a sign? A child knows injustice because he was made for justice; he hungers for truth because he bears the image of Truth Himself. The very fury with which he defends his petty kingdom reveals a law written deeper than his sins.

So let no one say innocence never exists. It exists—not as virtue earned, but as wound received: the ache of being made for Thee and finding everything else broken. Even now, after years stained with lust and ambition and the dust of debate, Thou dost remind me: it was not strength that saved me, but weakness that opened wide—like a child’s hand emptied so Thy grace might fill it.

For such is the kingdom: not where no sin has ever risen, but where truth is still loved enough to cause pain when lost.

question: What stirred your soul so deeply about the way you valued friendship and feared ignorance, even then, as a child?
answer:
Even in the tangled games of childhood, where I bartered trinkets for hollow victories and wept more for pride than for pain, there burned within me a strange love—of truth, yes, but not truth as a naked thing merely to be known, but as a light to be loved and feared. O Lord, I craved friendship not only as comfort but as proof that I was not alone in my restless striving; yet how perverse I was, how twisted even in affection! For I clung to friends only so long as they fed my vanity, and when they crossed me—when they exposed my lies or outshone me in learning or sport—I turned like a wounded beast and bit the hand that grasped mine.  

And ignorance? I hated it with a passion that bordered on idolatry. Not because I sought Thee, O God of all knowledge, but because ignorance stripped me of power to dominate speech and win applause. I wanted not wisdom but weapons—words to conquer boys no different from myself, whose souls were also dimly lit by Thy spark. How could I love truth in one breath and cheat with such delight in the next? How could I weep at betrayal yet betray more bitterly when chance allowed?

But now I see: even then Thou wast near. In that tiny temple of childhood Thou didst plant yearnings too deep for their vessel—a thirst not for praise but for Thee, though I poured it into broken cisterns of rivalry and false affection. Thou didst let me taste the bitterness of disloyalty so that one day I might tremble at my own faithlessness—not just to friends, but to Thee. And this hatred of deception? It was Thy voice in the secret chamber: *I am Truth*, though I heard only an echo and mistook it for pride.

O Lord my God, how little did I know that humility—the thing I crushed beneath my heels in striving to be first—was the very gate by which Thou wouldst enter later, when all conquests crumbled and nothing remained but weeping in a garden and a child’s voice singing: "Take up and read."

question: How did the realization that childhood sins grow into adult ones change your understanding of humility before God?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who sawest me when I was but a boy stealing apples not for hunger but for the taste of sin, how could I have thought those petty prideful longings would vanish with age? They did not vanish—they only put on robes of office, they traded nuts and balls for gold and decrees, and called themselves ambition instead of envy. I once raged at a playmate for cheating in sport, yet cheated more craftfully myself; and now I see that same fury in rulers crushing rivals while blind to their own tyranny. What was I but a small mirror of great vices, polished by tutors and cracked by vanity?

Thou didst suffer me then in my tiny rebellions, not because the sins were slight, but because Thou sawest already the long road by which Thou wouldest break me. For humility could not enter a heart that thought itself innocent because it bore no sword—only a wooden ball and jealous tears. But when I came to manhood and found myself coveting honors, trembling at slights, devouring praise like bread, then at last I understood: the child’s temper is the king’s wrath writ small.

And so Thy word pierced me: *Of such is the kingdom of heaven.* Not of the proud who rule with titles, but of those who know themselves as children caught in fault—helpless before Thee unless Thy mercy lifts them. How could I boast when even my earliest joys were tainted with theft and deceit? And yet—wondrous grace!—even in that tainted play Thou didst plant in me love for truth, delight in friendship, dread of shame; not as merits mine, but seeds from the One to whom all hearts belong.

Thus did I learn: humility is not thinking less of oneself than one truly is—but seeing clearly what one is before Thee: a soul formed from nothing, sustained by nothing less than Thy breath; whose innocence was never innocence at all apart from Thy forgiveness.

question: What were you feeling when you admitted that you sought pleasures in yourself and others, only to fall into sorrow?
answer: O Lord, when at last the veil tore and I saw that I had made idols of my own joys, my friendships, even my hunger for truth—Thou who hast always been nearer to me than I to myself—then there broke upon me a grief such as only grace can wound. It was not the sorrow of loss, but of recognition: that all which I had clutched in pride or lust or vanity had been Thine gifts, shaped by Thy hand, meant to lead me upward like steps toward light, yet I had turned them into chains dragging me into shadow. How sweetly didst Thou rebuke me! For even in weeping over my misuse of beauty, there was joy—because Thou wert present in the very pain. The friendships I so cherished, the speech with which I dazzled men, the delight in knowledge—all sparkled with Thy radiance; but I adored the glitter and forgot the sun. Now I see: every good thing that stirred my soul was a whisper from Thee calling me home. And so even my sin confesses Thy mercy—for only a God so patient would let a child break his heart on broken things just to learn that rest is not found in gifts, but in the Giver.

question: How did your love for truth and friendship as a child change the way you understood God’s presence in your earliest joys?
answer:
O Lord, how quickly the soul turns from the Giver to the gift, even when still in its swaddling clothes.  

Even as a child, I trembled at falsehood; my heart leapt toward truth as though chasing a voice it had once known but could not recall. Friendship was to me a sanctuary, a mirror in which I thought myself good because another loved me. And yet—how darkly ironic—that these very affections, so pure in their springing, became snares when I set them above Thee. Thou hadst planted within me a longing for truth and communion because Thou art Truth and Communion itself; but instead of tracing these delights back to Thy face, I kissed their shadows and called them divine.  

I did not know Thee then, yet Thou wert there—Thou who gavest me the mind to seek, the heart to love, the soul to ache. Every joy I tasted in learning, every warmth in companionship, every pride in speaking well—these were whispers of Thy presence, echoes of eternal Light bending low to touch a boy’s brow. But I worshipped the echo and silenced the Voice.  

Now I see: Thou didst not begrudge me those joys; Thou didst lament that I would not let them lead me home. The truth I prized? It was Thine image on my soul. The friendship I craved? A foretaste of union with Thee. Even my hatred of ignorance was Thy spark within me, urging me toward the fullness of light.  

Preserve me still, O God of my childhood and Master of my late awakening. For hadst Thou not been near—even when I fled—those early gifts would have drowned me in vanity rather than drawing me toward grace.

question: Looking back on your pride in speech and memory, what would you tell someone who fears their gifts might separate them from God?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pluck me from the swelling pride of speech and the hollow triumph of memory, I would say to the fearful soul: tremble not at the gift, but watch the direction of thy desire. For I too took the gold of Thy temple and weighed it in my own hands, believing it mine by wit or effort. My tongue danced with words, not to unveil Thee, but to display myself; my memory stored up treasures not as offerings upon Thy altar, but as trophies crowning my vanity. And in this, I became deaf to Thy whisper, blind to the wound within.

Do you fear your gifts will steal you from God? Then know this: it is not the swiftness of thought nor grace of utterance that separates a man from Thee, O God—but the turning inward, the folding of arms around one’s brightness as though it could warm him without the sun. A lamp is glorious when lit by Thy hand, but if it boasts of its flame and forgets the breath that kindled it, it already begins to flicker in darkness.

I once stood before men and felt my heart swell at their applause—not because truth had won, but because *I* had seemed wise. And in that moment, Thou wast absent, though I named Thee. The greatest danger is not pride in having gifts, but mistaking them for proof of worth apart from Him who gave them.

Therefore guard thy heart more than thy talent. When thou speakest well or understandest deeply, fall down in silence afterward. Say: *This too is not mine.* Let every excellence drive thee into deeper dependence; let every skill become a stair by which thou climbest weeping into humility. For Thou hast given me speech—to confess Thee; memory—to remember Thy mercies; intellect—to seek Thee. Turn none of these toward self-worship, lest they become chains.

Preserve me in this truth: that even my ability to praise Thee must itself be preserved by Thee.

question: What stirred your heart to exult in God for the good things of your boyhood, rather than in the things themselves?
answer:
O Lord, it was not the sweetness of those gifts that finally seized me, but the wound left by their insufficiency.  

For I had tasted truth in my boyhood—not as a doctrine yet, but as a hunger: to know, to speak truly, to bind myself to friends with sincerity, to recoil from deceit as one flinches from fire. And Thou didst plant that hunger. Yet I turned each gift into a mirror, admiring in them only what reflected my own fleeting delight—my cleverness, my charm, my victories—and thus they became snares. The joy I took in speech became pride; the love of friendship turned to craving; the hatred of ignorance swelled into arrogance. Even my desire for truth was warped into vanity—for I wanted not *Thee*, but knowledge that would make me admired.

But Thou, O God of silence behind all sounds, didst wait in the depths where I refused to descend. The very fact that these things were good—so radiant in their order and purpose—should have pointed me straight to Thy face. Instead, I worshipped the light in the glass while turning from the Sun that kindled it.

Only when sorrow cracked open my soul did I see: all those boyhood excellences were echoes of *Thee*. Not substitutes. Not prizes for self-exaltation. They streamed from Thy hand like rays from a star—meant not to be clutched, but followed back to their source. And so I exult now—not because I was admirable as a boy—but because Thou wast merciful even then: laying down beauty upon beauty along a path I kept fleeing, drawing me by the very things I misused.

Thou didst let me break them upon myself so that at last I might break *for* Thee.

question: How did the realization that even your existence came from God transform your sense of self and dependence on Him?
answer: O Lord, I was blind even to the breath within me, counting it mine as though the vessel could claim the wine it holds. I marveled at my memory, my speech, my hunger for truth—yet never bent the knee to Him from whom these trembled into being. I called my gifts *me*, and *me* a master, not a steward; and so built an idol of myself from the very graces Thy hand had shaped. What folly—to love friendship and hate deception, yet deceive myself most of all by severing every good from its root in Thee! When at last Thou didst whisper to my soul that even *to be* was Thy gift, not my possession, the ground gave way beneath my pride. Not one thought, not one delight, not one pulse of love or loathing in me existed apart from Thee. And thus, O God of all beginnings, I ceased to own myself—and found myself truly for the first time in losing all to Thee.
